passages.explanation: The church in Antioch has a diverse leadership team: Barnabas, Simeon called Niger (likely of African descent), Lucius of Cyrene (North Africa), Manaen (who grew up with Herod the tetrarch — a man from the royal court), and Saul. While they're worshipping and fasting, the Holy Spirit says: set apart Barnabas and Saul for the work I have called them to. After fasting and prayer, they lay hands on them and send them off. This is the beginning of intentional missionary activity — the church doesn't just scatter accidentally, it now sends deliberately.

Barnabas and Saul, sent out by the Holy Spirit, sail to Cyprus — Barnabas' home island. John Mark accompanies them as their assistant. They preach in the Jewish synagogues across the island. In Paphos they encounter Bar-Jesus (also called Elymas), a Jewish sorcerer and false prophet attached to the Roman proconsul Sergius Paulus, an intelligent man who wants to hear the word of God. Elymas tries to turn the proconsul away from the faith. Saul — now called Paul for the first time — filled with the Holy Spirit, looks straight at Elymas and denounces him as a child of the devil and enemy of righteousness. Paul declares him temporarily blind. Mist and darkness fall on him. The proconsul sees this and believes, amazed at the teaching about the Lord. From this point Luke switches from 'Saul' to 'Paul' — his Roman name for his mission to the Roman world.

From Paphos, Paul and his companions sail to Perga in Pamphylia, where John Mark leaves them and returns to Jerusalem. Luke doesn't explain why, but Paul will later consider it desertion (chapter 15). They continue to Pisidian Antioch and go to the synagogue on the Sabbath. After the reading of the Law and the Prophets, the synagogue leaders invite them to speak if they have a word of encouragement. This is Paul's opportunity — visiting teachers were regularly given the floor.

Paul stands and addresses the congregation — both Jews and God-fearing Gentiles. He begins with a sweep through Israel's history: God chose their ancestors, made them great during their stay in Egypt, led them through the wilderness, gave them the land of Canaan, provided judges, then King Saul, and then David — a man after God's own heart. From David's descendants, God has brought to Israel the Saviour Jesus, as he promised. John the Baptist prepared the way, proclaiming a baptism of repentance and pointing beyond himself to the one coming after him. Paul is building his case on the foundation of Jewish history and scripture.

Paul addresses the audience directly: this message of salvation has been sent to us. He explains that the people of Jerusalem and their rulers didn't recognise Jesus and condemned him, fulfilling the prophets' words that are read every Sabbath. Though they found no grounds for a death sentence, they asked Pilate to have him executed. When they'd carried out everything written about him, they took him down from the cross and laid him in a tomb. But God raised him from the dead, and for many days he was seen by those who had travelled with him from Galilee to Jerusalem — they are now his witnesses. Paul declares: what God promised our ancestors he has fulfilled for us by raising Jesus. He cites Psalm 2, Isaiah 55, and Psalm 16 to prove that God's promise of resurrection applies to Jesus, not David — because David died and decayed, but Jesus did not see decay.

Paul reaches the heart of his message: through Jesus, forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you. Through him everyone who believes is set free from every sin — something the law of Moses could never do. This is the core Pauline message: the law couldn't justify, but faith in Jesus can. He closes with a warning from Habakkuk: look, you scoffers, wonder and perish — I am going to do something in your days that you would never believe, even if someone told you. The warning is pointed: don't dismiss what God is doing right in front of you.

As Paul and Barnabas leave the synagogue, the people invite them to speak again the next Sabbath. Many Jews and God-fearing converts follow them and are urged to continue in God's grace. The next Sabbath, almost the whole city gathers to hear the word of the Lord. When the Jewish leaders see the crowds, they're filled with jealousy and begin contradicting Paul and slandering him. The opposition isn't theological — it's territorial. The crowds are the problem.

Paul and Barnabas respond boldly: it was necessary to speak the word of God to you first. Since you reject it and don't consider yourselves worthy of eternal life, we're turning to the Gentiles. They quote Isaiah 49:6 — God appointed them as a light for the Gentiles, to bring salvation to the ends of the earth. The Gentiles are glad and honour the word of the Lord, and all who are appointed for eternal life believe. The word of the Lord spreads through the whole region. But the Jewish leaders stir up influential women and leading men to persecute Paul and Barnabas and drive them out. They shake the dust from their feet in protest and go to Iconium. The disciples are filled with joy and the Holy Spirit. A pattern is established that will repeat throughout Acts: preach to Jews first, face rejection, turn to Gentiles, get expelled, move on.
